track.title: Farmer Seasonal Money Plan
track.description: A practical route for farmers managing seasonal income, input costs, emergency buffers, and cautious investment learning.

course.title: Farmer Seasonal Money Plan: Core route
course.description: Plan harvest income without confusing working capital, emergency money, and longer-term investing.

lessons.title: Seasonal income is not monthly salary
lessons.summary: Farm income often arrives in chunks, so the plan must protect the next season before investing.
lessons.introduction: A farmer may receive money after harvest, livestock sale, milk payments, produce deliveries, or cooperative payouts. Because the next income date can be uncertain, the first job is to protect input money, household needs, and emergency cash before considering longer-term routes.
sections: Seasonal income should be divided by purpose, not treated as surplus on the day it arrives.
Input money for seeds, feed, fertilizer, transport, labour, or veterinary costs should stay liquid enough for the next cycle.
Weather, market price, pests, transport, and buyer delays make emergency funds especially important.

scenario.title: Harvest payout
scenario.text: A farmer receives KES 80,000 after selling produce. They first separate next-season inputs, school fees, household needs, and only then a learning amount.

lessons.mistake: Putting next-season input money into an illiquid route and later borrowing expensively to farm again.
lessons.action: Split one harvest amount into input reserve, household reserve, emergency reserve, debt obligations, and learning/investment range.
lessons.takeaway: Protect the next season before chasing returns.
lessons.source_note: Source note: This is educational planning; verify product details with official/provider sources.

lessons.title: Liquidity for input cycles
lessons.summary: Learn why money needed soon should stay easy to access and clearly labelled.
lessons.introduction: Liquidity means how quickly money can be accessed without major delay, penalty, or loss. For a farmer, liquidity is not an abstract finance word: it can determine whether inputs are bought on time before rains or before a buyer payment clears.
sections: Near-season money should be separate from long-term money.
A Money Market Fund may be useful for some short-term learning scenarios, but withdrawal time and fees must be checked.
Longer maturity routes can create stress if money is needed for planting, feed, repairs, or transport.

scenario.title: Fertilizer money locked away
scenario.text: A farmer invests money needed in six weeks into a route they cannot exit quickly. They later borrow at high cost for inputs.

lessons.mistake: Choosing a route before matching it to the actual date money is needed.
lessons.action: Run an MMF simulator for a short-term input reserve and write the withdrawal-time assumption.
lessons.source_note: Source note: Check actual withdrawal terms with each provider; PesaRoute does not execute investments.

lessons.title: Cooperatives, SACCOs, and group money
lessons.summary: Farmers may use SACCOs, cooperatives, or groups; records and rules matter.
lessons.introduction: Many farmers interact with cooperatives, SACCOs, table-banking groups, or producer groups. These can be useful structures, but the learner should read rules, verify status where relevant, and understand deposits, shares, loans, guarantors, and withdrawal limits.
sections: SACCO deposits and share capital can have different withdrawal and ownership rules.
Loan access can create guarantor obligations or deductions from future income.
Group records should clearly show contributions, withdrawals, fines, loans, and decisions.

scenario.title: Milk payout and SACCO loan
scenario.text: A dairy farmer considers increasing SACCO deposits. They check deposit rules, loan deductions, and emergency cash needs first.

lessons.mistake: Joining or borrowing through a group without reading contribution, withdrawal, and guarantor rules.
lessons.action: Write down the group or SACCO rule you understand least and ask for clarification before increasing contributions.
lessons.takeaway: Group money needs written rules, not assumptions.
lessons.source_note: Source note: Verify regulated SACCO status and rules with official/provider documents.

lessons.title: Farmer journal after payout
lessons.summary: Record harvest decisions, expected next costs, and what must remain liquid.
lessons.introduction: A seasonal money journal helps the farmer remember the reasoning behind each split. It can record the harvest amount as exact, rounded, range, or hidden, then focus on what money must remain available before the next income cycle.
sections: A good entry lists next-season costs before optional investing.
The user should include due dates: school fees, rent, inputs, debt, animal health, or transport.
Range or hidden amount mode can protect privacy while still teaching the planning habit.

scenario.title: Before spending begins
scenario.text: After receiving a payout, a farmer creates a note: input money locked? no, school fees due date, SACCO contribution amount, emergency reserve range.

lessons.mistake: Waiting until money is finished before writing what happened.
lessons.action: Create one journal note called 'next season protection' and list the first three bills due.
lessons.takeaway: A written split reduces pressure spending after harvest.
lessons.source_note: Source note: This is education only and does not replace agricultural, legal, tax, or financial advice.

quiz_questions.prompt: What should be checked before increasing SACCO or group contributions?
quiz_questions.explanation: Written rules affect access and risk.

quiz_questions.prompt: What should happen before choosing a route for input money?
options: Match route to date money is needed
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Match route to date money is needed

quiz_questions.explanation: Input reserve, household needs, and learning money should not be mixed blindly.

flashcards.back: Money set aside for the next farming cycle.
flashcards.example: Seeds, feed, fertilizer, labour, repairs, or transport.

flashcards.back: How quickly money can be accessed without major delay or loss.
flashcards.example: Input money due next month should stay easy to access.

flashcards.back: The risk of being responsible if another borrower fails to pay.
flashcards.example: A SACCO guarantor can face deductions.

flashcards.back: They show decisions, obligations, and accountability.
To record the plan before pressure spending begins.
flashcards.example: Write next input due dates and emergency reserve.

resources.title: Farmer seasonal money checklist
resources.body: A checklist for farmers after seasonal income arrives: protect input money, list household obligations, keep emergency cash liquid, verify group rules, and use simulators only for education.
items: List dates for school fees, rent, debt, transport, and feed.
Check SACCO, cooperative, or group rules before adding more money.
